title: RFC An approach examining NGE in the context of real life
display_title: RFC An approach examining NGE in the context of real life
wikitext:
[]: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real life             Quote Post by Curious Case of Curiosity » Wed Jan 23, 2013 8:45 pm A month ago, I outlined in my first post on this website how I would be presenting a different approach to analyzing the fictional existence that is the Neon Genesis Evangelion franchise. It is still being developed, but I feel like testing the waters here with a request for comments. Briefly, I will list several points that will persist throughout my post on the topic: Meaning is created through intent. The creation of the content we consume when we take in (i.e., read, watch, listen, etc.) a particular medium has a causal relationship with real events. Many interpretations of a work may exist, but whatever a work's creator's interpretation—intent, if you will—happens to be, it is a fact that is indeed their interpretation. By examining the way a person might think provides insight into their frame of mind and their intent for certain topics that come up for debate. So, please, if I may oblige you, voice your thoughts in a reply to this thread.

[]: Re: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real li             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jan 23, 2013 9:20 pm Meaning is created through intent.

[]: Re: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real li             Quote Post by Curious Case of Curiosity » Wed Jan 23, 2013 9:45 pm NemZ wrote: No, intent creates content. Meaning is only created through understanding of that content.

[]: Re: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real li             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jan 24, 2013 12:18 am author(s) » meaning » intent » content » audience » meaning(s) Curious Case of Curiosity wrote: To dismiss the notion that the topic of discussion is not subjective.

[]: Re: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real li             Quote Post by Curious Case of Curiosity » Thu Jan 24, 2013 3:24 pm NemZ wrote: author(s) » meaning » intent » content » audience » meaning(s)

[]: Re: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real li             Quote Post by pwhodges » Thu Jan 24, 2013 3:41 pm Curious Case of Curiosity wrote: The audience derives its meaning in an effort to reconstruct the author's meaning.

[]: Re: RFC: An approach examining NGE in the context of real li             Quote Post by Curious Case of Curiosity » Thu Jan 24, 2013 6:57 pm pwhodges wrote: It is fashionable, I believe, to derive the meaning you find satisfactory without reference to what might be thought to be the author's intended meaning - or at least to give the author's view of their own intended meaning no more weight than the audience's. Personally I think that is somewhat arrogant and also a bit of a cop-out, but then I don't move in circles that are engaged in literary criticism. Of course, much of the time there is no access to the author's thought other than the work itself, in which case that's the best that can be done.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Jan 24, 2013 7:07 pm I don't even understand what the OP is trying to say. All I'm getting out of this is "written works mean things that the author was thinking at some point." Um....yea, and?

[]:             Quote Post by Curious Case of Curiosity » Thu Jan 24, 2013 7:25 pm AuraTwilight wrote: I don't even understand what the OP is trying to say. All I'm getting out of this is "written works mean things that the author was thinking at some point." Um....yea, and?

[]:             Quote Post by jimmiel » Thu Jan 24, 2013 8:08 pm I don't personally like the "Death of the Author" idea, but I can't deny it has merit. Garzey's Wing's English dub is absolutely a work of comedy, it just happens to be unintentionally so. The more intelligent a writer and the better-written a text, the more weight their not-implicitly-meaningful view on it gains. The author has - in theory - a better idea of what went into the making of a text, and is therefore in a better position to say what came out of it.
